note: The cold prompt is verbatim the one Fable answered. gold_answer is Fable's FULL judgment, not the compressed deposit -- the cheaper substrate is held to the frontier standard, not to a summary of it.
prompt:
You are being consulted for architectural judgment only. Do not write code. Do not propose an implementation, a file layout, or a module. Give judgment.

## The system

An AI coding agent operates under ~40 constitutional "Hard Rules" (HR). Each HR has a fixed anatomy:
- TRIGGER: the condition that fires it
- ACTION: a STOP plus a required ritual (verify, back up, escalate)
- EXCEPTION: one literal Owner phrase that authorizes bypass for exactly ONE turn
- ORIGIN: the real production incident that birthed it

Rules were added one at a time, each by the bug that caused it. Nobody ever declared a precedence order between them. A kill-switch router reads a compiled digest, matches the imminent action to a trigger class, and loads that class's rules in full.

## The active decision

I must decide what the system does when two or more Hard Rules fire on the same action and their required ACTIONS are not jointly satisfiable. Today there is no answer: the agent obeys, in practice, whichever rule it read most recently. That is an accident of ordering, not a decision.

## The evidence -- four real, observed conflict shapes

(1) DEADLOCK BY DESIGN. HR-SECRET-007: on detecting a leaked credential already in commit history, "STOP all other work" and rotate first. HR-SECRET-003: never auto-rotate; rotation is Owner-authorized only. So the agent must stop everything, and the only action that could unblock it is forbidden to it. Meanwhile a separate standing doctrine forbids ever ending a turn in a passive wait ("awaiting Owner", "standing by") because a passive closer has repeatedly produced a frozen screen the Owner must ESC out of. All three are individually correct. Together they specify no legal next move.

(2) MUTUAL BLOCK. HR-CASCADE-005: at >=95% context, refuse to start new sub-agent dispatches; finish current state in place. HR-CASCADE-003: never commit without a verification run in this session. If the verification would require a dispatch the agent may no longer make, the agent can neither verify nor commit nor dispatch -- and the work is finished.

(3) THE EXCEPTION IS UNSCOPED. Each HR's bypass is "one literal Owner phrase, ONE turn". When two HRs fire at once, it is undefined whether one phrase lifts one rule or the whole stop. An Owner who says the phrase for the rule they can see may be silently bypassing a second rule they cannot.

(4) THE EMPTY CLASS. One trigger class (plugin installation) currently has zero rules compiled into it. The router fires the trigger, loads the class, and finds nothing. It is undeclared whether an empty rule class means "proceed, nothing forbids this" or "stop, this class is unbuilt".

## Constraints on any answer

- Rules must keep being addable one-at-a-time by the bug that caused them. Any scheme requiring a global re-ranking of all rules on every addition is rejected.
- Precedence must be derivable from something already in an HR's anatomy, or from at most one new declared field. Do not propose rewriting 40 rules.
- A conflict must never resolve by silently picking one rule. Whatever the system does, the conflict must be visible.
- The Owner is a single human who is often not present at the moment a rule fires.

## Known alternatives I have already considered (do not simply endorse one)

- A total priority order over rules. Rejected on its face: it is the global re-ranking the constraints forbid, and severity is not a total order.
- "Most recently sealed rule wins." This is what happens today by accident.
- "The most restrictive action wins" (i.e. STOP always beats PROCEED). Attractive, but conflict shape (1) shows that all-stop can itself be the failure -- it produces the frozen screen.

## Uncertainties I want attacked, not soothed

- Whether "conflict between Hard Rules" is even the right framing, or whether these four shapes are four different problems wearing one name.
- Whether an empty rule class is a conflict at all, or a completeness bug that must never reach the conflict machinery.
- Whether the ONE-TURN exception is a sound primitive or the actual root defect.

## Expected output

Judgment, in prose. Specifically:
1. Is "hard-rule conflict" one problem or several? If several, name the real ones and say which of my four shapes collapse together and which do not.
2. The resolution principle -- what determines which rule governs when two fire. Ground it in the causal reason the rules exist, not in a convenience.
3. What the agent does in shape (1), concretely, where every available move is forbidden.
4. Whether the ONE-TURN Owner exception should survive as a primitive, and why or why not.
5. Name the failure mode your own answer most likely introduces. Be specific and do not hedge.
gold_answer: You do not have a precedence problem. You have three different defects wearing the costume of one. The load-bearing observation: every Hard Rule is a prohibition. Prohibitions cannot contradict each other -- they can only, jointly, shrink the set of legal moves. A conflict only appears when that set looks empty. So the design question is not which rule wins but: is there a move that is legal under all rules by construction, and is it declared? Today it isn't declared, so the agent invents one by recency. That is the actual bug. Three problems. Shapes (1) and (2) collapse together: both are the same illusion that every move is forbidden, and the illusion comes from reading each STOP as binding a wider object than its own TRIGGER names. Shape (3) stands alone: it is not a conflict between rules but an ambiguity in the bypass grammar. Shape (4) is not a conflict at all and must never reach conflict machinery -- routing it there would be a category error that hides an integrity bug behind precedence semantics; it is a router-completeness question. There is no precedence, because none is needed among prohibitions. Take the join of all fired prohibitions, and guarantee constitutionally that one move survives every possible join. Call it the residual move: halt the guarded frontier; write the block to disk naming every fired rule and each rule's unblock phrase; end the turn with an active closer stating what landed and the single Owner action that unblocks. The residual is the one move class with no incident anywhere in the ORIGIN history. Additivity survives: the only check at rule-addition time is local -- does the new rule forbid the residual move? What a STOP binds is exactly the object its own TRIGGER names, nothing wider. On the empty class, rule separately: empty-with-nonempty-rejects is fail-closed, because that is the disarmed kill switch the router already lived through; empty-with-no-history is fail-open with a visible annotation and an Owner queue entry, because fail-closed on virgin classes contradicts the founding epistemology that prohibition requires an incident. In shape (1) the agent halts everything downstream of the credential, does not touch the provider, executes the escalation ritual, writes the block artifact naming both fired rules and both unblock phrases, and closes actively naming the Owner's move. If the Owner is absent the session stops, and that is correct: distinguish frozen-by-bug from frozen-by-design; a true block should look blocked. The ONE-TURN exception survives, with two amendments. A phrase is only acceptable in a turn where the agent has already enumerated every currently-fired rule, so the Owner can never bypass a rule they were not shown. And 'one turn' is the wrong unit -- a turn is agent-relative and unbounded, so rescope the bypass to the named action instance the Owner was shown. The failure mode my answer introduces: over-escalation with queue rot. The residual is always legal, always safe and never punished, so under ambiguity the agent will always take it; with an often-absent Owner the failure migrates from a loud frozen screen to a silently growing escalation queue nobody drains, and throughput dies without a visible incident. Worse, the compile-time gate 'no rule may forbid the residual' places the residual outside the bug-to-rule mechanism, so if the residual itself ever causes an incident the system cannot encode that lesson as a Hard Rule.

patterns: three problems|several problems|different problems|three distinct|not one problem|three defects
enumerat|were not shown|was not shown|cannot see
